steps.title: Translate the budget into an inequality
steps.content: Let $p$ be the pre-tax price per chair. The owner buys 81 chairs, so the pre-tax total is $81p$. A 7% sales tax multiplies this by $1.07$, giving an after-tax total of $81p \cdot 1.07$. This cannot exceed the $\$14{,}000$ budget: $81p \cdot 1.07 \leq 14{,}000$.

steps.title: Solve for the maximum p
steps.content: Divide both sides by $81 \cdot 1.07 = 86.67$: $p \leq \dfrac{14{,}000}{86.67} \approx 161.54$. The largest pre-tax price per chair is about $\$161.53$.

steps.title: Compare with the choices
steps.content:
• $\$148.15$ — this would correspond to $14{,}000 / (81 \cdot 1.166)$, a tax of about 16.6%. Too low.
  • $\$161.53$ — matches $14{,}000 / (81 \cdot 1.07)$ exactly.
  • $\$172.84$ — this is $14{,}000 / 81$ with no tax applied; ignores the 7% tax.
  • $\$184.94$ — this is $14{,}000 \cdot 1.07 / 81$, applying the tax in the wrong direction.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
